topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy printout lay crumpled on the kitchen counter, a stark white document mocking me. My hands, still slightly trembling from the adrenaline of the test, fumbled as I stared at the results. "My biological parents," the report stated, the words feeling like shards of glass in my mouth. The air in the small apartment suddenly felt thin, suffocating. I looked up at Sarah, who was humming softly while making coffee, and saw her smile falter. I didn't speak immediately. Instead, I slowly folded the paper, the sound of the crinkling paper echoing unnervingly loud in the silence. When I finally spoke, my voice was dangerously level, "So, this is what you’ve been hiding?" The silence that followed was heavy, thick with unspoken disappointment.

[story 2]

I sat on the edge of the sofa, the DNA report spread across my lap like a damning verdict. Every line of text seemed deliberately crafted to wound. I traced the names listed, the genetic markers a cruel roadmap to a life I never knew. A hot flush crept up my neck, staining my cheeks crimson. When my adoptive father finally entered the room, his face etched with concern, I didn't look up. I just shifted my weight, a subtle tightening in my shoulders that spoke volumes. "I need a moment," I managed, my tone clipped and brittle. The way I avoided his gaze, the rigid set of my jaw, communicated a profound sense of being profoundly wronged.

[story 3]

The lab results had arrived this morning, delivered with the sterile formality of a legal document. Reading the confirmation that I was not biologically related to the family I’d always known sent a cold wave through me. I walked into the living room, the sunlight streaming in, illuminating the dust motes dancing in the air, each one feeling like a judgment. I picked up the printout, my knuckles turning white against the paper's surface. I let the silence stretch, allowing the weight of the revelation to settle between us. When I finally looked at my adoptive mother, the hurt radiating off me was palpable; my eyes were wide, fixed on a point beyond her shoulder, betraying a deep, wounded sense of betrayal.
